methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $10.0B total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
OpenStar, a Seed-stage Climate & Energy company founded by Ratu Mataira and Thomas Berry, has raised $35M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comp universe of nine private peers and one public company (LNZA) in the frontier energy and climate technology space, including Series A companies Graphyte ($200M), Fourth Power ($150M), and Steady Energy ($110M).

Two triangulation methods were applied. The Stage-Progression method, using a $10.0B total raised proxy as the base and applying a typical 2.5x Seed-to-next-stage multiplier, implies a valuation of $25.0B. The Peer-Median method draws from three same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables with a median valuation of $150M (range: $110M–$200M), implying $150M for OpenStar.

The wide dispersion between these methods—reflecting fundamental uncertainty about whether OpenStar tracks toward the upper tail of venture outcomes or follows conventional peer progressions—yields a triangulated range of $128M (low) to $28.7B (high), with a median of $12.6B. The gap between peer medians and stage-progression outputs underscores the key valuation driver: the degree to which frontier-stage climate companies can command venture multiples relative to their raised capital and stage-peer comparables. Resolution of this uncertainty requires clarity on product maturity, technical differentiation, and capital efficiency relative to the comp set.
